# Ezra 6: The God Who Works Through Kings

The scroll recovered from Babylon's archives contains a decree that should have settled everything—Cyrus authorized the temple's rebuilding decades earlier. Yet opposition has delayed the work, and now Darius himself must intervene. What strikes us is not simply that a pagan king issues orders in God's favor, but *how* he does it. Darius doesn't merely grant permission; he commands resources from the royal treasury, mandates daily provisions for sacrifices, and threatens brutal punishment for anyone who interferes. Most remarkably, he invokes God's name and asks Him to judge any king or people who violate the decree. A ruler who worships other gods speaks with reverence about the God of Israel and His dwelling place. This is not coincidence. Behind Darius's words stands the sovereign hand of God, turning the heart of a powerful king toward the fulfillment of His purposes.

The Jewish elders built and prospered—but notice that verse 14 attributes their success to something beyond Darius's decree. The prophesying of Haggai and Zechariah sustained them. God spoke through His prophets while kings made their pronouncements. Both were necessary. The work advanced not because political protection removed all obstacles, but because God's people continued to obey His Word and respond to His voice through the prophets. They finished according to multiple commands: God's command to Israel, Cyrus's decree, Darius's decree, even Artaxerxes' support later. This layering of authorities teaches us something crucial—God accomplishes His will through many means, and His people must honor legitimate authority while remaining ultimately accountable to Him alone. The completion date itself is recorded with precision: the third day of Adar in Darius's sixth year. God's promises are not vague; they come to pass in time and space, in history that can be verified.

The dedication reveals what mattered most. The people brought offerings totaling eight hundred animals and twelve goats representing all twelve tribes—restored Israel, whole and complete before God. They appointed the priests and Levites according to what is written in the Book of Moses, restoring proper worship in its ordained forms. Then they celebrated Passover, that ancient remembrance of God's salvation from Egypt. Notice who participated: "the Israelites who had returned from exile ate it, together with all who had separated themselves from the uncleanness of the peoples of the land to seek the LORD." Worship and identity were bound together. To eat the Passover meant you had separated yourself from idolatry and sought the God of Israel. The sevenfold joy of Unleavened Bread was not mere emotion but gratitude—"the LORD had made them joyful." This joy was real because God had acted: He turned the king's heart, strengthened their hands, brought them through exile, and restored His house.

What you face in this chapter is the reality of a God who keeps His word across generations and works through the most unlikely instruments. The exiles had waited seventy years. They had watched their grandparents die in captivity. Then through patience, prayer, the voice of prophets, and the sovereign turning of kings' hearts, they witnessed the fulfillment of what God promised. Their joy at the dedication was not naive optimism but the joy of those who have seen God's faithfulness tested and proven true. If you find yourself in a place of waiting, of work that seems opposed, of purposes that appear delayed—this chapter asks you to trust that God is not passive. He is actively at work, even when the machinery of history seems to grind slowly. Return to His Word. Respond to His voice through Scripture. Honor the legitimate authorities He has placed over you. And wait to see how He will accomplish what He has promised. His timing is not our timing, but His word will be fulfilled.
